content.problemStatement:
You are given an N×MN \times MN×M matrix where each element of the matrix consists of an integer color. Let A[i][j]A[i][j]A[i][j] be the color on the matrix in the iii-th row from the top and jjj-th column from the left. You need to make the matrix beautiful.

By definition, a matrix is beautiful if all the elements of the iii-th row have color iii.
That is, all the elements of the first row have color 111, all elements of the second row have color 222, so on.

In an operation, you can do the following:


Choose four integers x1,y1,x2,y2x_1, y_1, x_2, y_2x1​,y1​,x2​,y2​ (1≤x1,x2≤N1\le x_1, x_2\le N1≤x1​,x2​≤N, 1≤y1,y2≤M1\le y_1, y_2\le M1≤y1​,y2​≤M, x1≠x2,y1≠y2x_1\ne x_2, y_1\ne y_2x1​=x2​,y1​=y2​)
Swap the values A[x1][y1]A[x_1][y_1]A[x1​][y1​] and A[x2][y1]A[x_2][y_1]A[x2​][y1​].
Swap the values A[x1][y2]A[x_1][y_2]A[x1​][y2​] and A[x2][y2]A[x_2][y_2]A[x2​][y2​].
If you can make the matrix beautiful, then give a sequence of at most (N+3)⋅(M+3)(N+3)\cdot (M+3)(N+3)⋅(M+3) operations to do so. Otherwise, print −1-1−1.

We have a proof that if it is possible to make the matrix beautiful in a finite number of operations, then it can be done in at most (N+3)⋅(M+3)(N+3)\cdot (M+3)(N+3)⋅(M+3) operations.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains two integers NNN and MMM.
The next NNN lines have MMM integers each. The jjj-th integer in the iii-th line denotes A[i][j]A[i][j]A[i][j]


Output Format
For each test case:



If it is possible to make the matrix beautiful:

Print the number of operations you perform in a new line. This number has to be less than or equal to (N+3)⋅(M+3)(N+3) \cdot (M+3)(N+3)⋅(M+3).
For each operation print four integers: x1x_1x1​, y1y_1y1​, x2x_2x2​, y2y_2y2​ in a new line.


If it is not possible to make the matrix beautiful:

Print −1-1−1 in a new line.



If there are multiple ways to make the matrix beautiful with at most (N+3)⋅(M+3)(N+3) \cdot (M+3)(N+3)⋅(M+3) operations, you may print any of them.
constraints: Each column contains a permutation of the integers 1,…,N1,\ldots, N1,…,N.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
For the first test case, we can swap A[2][2],A[3][2]A[2][2], A[3][2]A[2][2],A[3][2] and swap A[2][3],A[3][3]A[2][3],A[3][3]A[2][3],A[3][3] to make the matrix beautiful.
For the second test case it is not possible to make the matrix beautiful.
